military_service_please_introduce_us_to_:
I enlisted in the U.S. Army in the spring of 2004, about two and a half years after the 9/11 attacks. I was 20 years old, from Dayton, Ohio, and like a lot of people my age at the time, I felt a strong pull to serve after watching the towers fall when I was a senior in high school. I shipped out to basic training at Fort Benning, GA, in June 2004 and completed infantry OSUT that fall. My official dates of active duty service were from 2004-06-14 to 2012-09-30.

After basic and airborne school, I was assigned to the 82nd Airborne Division at Fort Bragg (now Fort Liberty), North Carolina. I served primarily as an infantryman (11B), later promoted to squad leader and then platoon sergeant by the end of my service. My first deployment was to Iraq from 2005-09-03 to 2006-11-18, operating mostly in and around Baghdad and Baqubah as part of stability and counterinsurgency operations.

During that first deployment, most of what we dealt with were IEDs, small-arms fire, and the constant grind of patrols. We did route clearance, presence patrols, and partnered with Iraqi Army units. While it was stressful and we had a few close calls, I was not physically injured on that tour. I did start to experience some sleep issues and anxiety, but at the time I just chalked it up to the deployment tempo and tried to push through it.

My second deployment was to Afghanistan from 2008-03-10 to 2009-04-02. We were based out of FOB Fenty in Nangarhar Province and conducted operations throughout the surrounding valleys. Our mission focused on disrupting insurgent supply lines and supporting local Afghan National Army and Afghan National Police units. I was a team leader during this rotation and later took over as acting squad leader when my squad leader rotated home early due to family issues.

I was injured on this Afghanistan deployment on 2008-09-21. We were on a mounted patrol in a three-vehicle convoy conducting a route clearance and presence patrol along a frequently targeted stretch of road near the Pakistan border. I was in the lead vehicle, a Mine-Resistant Ambush Protected (MRAP) truck, serving as the vehicle commander.

At approximately 0930 local time, our vehicle hit a pressure-plate IED that was buried on the shoulder of the road. The blast detonated under the front portion of the vehicle, slightly off-center. I remember a bright flash, an intense upward jolt, and then a kind of slow-motion feeling as my head snapped forward and then back against the seat. The MRAP did its job in the sense that it kept us alive, but the force of the explosion still threw us around inside the vehicle.

In the immediate aftermath, I was disoriented and had ringing in my ears. I had a pounding headache and felt like I couldn’t quite focus my eyes. I also felt a sharp pain in my lower back and a burning sensation along my left leg. Our driver had shrapnel in his arm, and the gunner was knocked unconscious briefly when his helmet hit the turret ring. We pulled security and waited for the QRF and medevac.

I was evacuated to FOB Fenty’s medical facility later that morning. The initial diagnosis was a mild traumatic brain injury (concussion) from the blast and a lumbar strain. Over the next 48 hours, I developed worsening headaches, sensitivity to light and noise, nausea, and trouble concentrating. I also had numbness and tingling down my left leg. X-rays didn’t show any fractures, but an MRI later suggested a herniated disc at L4-L5.

I stayed at FOB Fenty for about a week for observation and then was sent to Bagram for further evaluation. Ultimately, I was not sent all the way back to the States at that time, but I was placed on light duty for several weeks and pulled from patrols. I returned to my unit in a limited capacity, doing more operations room work, radio watch, and logistics tasks rather than regular combat patrols. I completed the remainder of the deployment with restrictions on heavy lifting and wearing full kit for extended periods.

When I returned to Fort Bragg in April 2009, I continued to have chronic lower back pain, especially after ruck marches, airborne operations, or long periods of standing. I also started to notice cognitive and emotional changes that I didn’t fully understand at the time: difficulty concentrating, short-term memory problems, irritability, and what I now recognize as anxiety and hypervigilance. I brushed most of it off and tried to stay in the fight, but it definitely affected my performance and my relationships.

In 2010, I was promoted to Staff Sergeant (E-6) and took over a squad. My third deployment was again to Afghanistan, this time from 2010-06-05 to 2011-05-20, operating in Kunar Province. By then, the back issues and headaches were more constant, but I felt a strong sense of responsibility to my guys and didn’t want to be the one sitting out. I continued to jump and patrol but had to rely heavily on pain medication and physical therapy exercises.

During that third deployment, I had a second significant incident related to my earlier injuries. On 2010-11-03, we were dismounting from a helo insertion onto a mountainside LZ. I misjudged the step, landed awkwardly on loose rock with a full combat load, and felt a sharp, tearing pain in my lower back that dropped me to a knee. I managed to finish the movement, but by the time we got back to the COP, I could barely bend or twist. Medical evaluated me and tied it back to the preexisting disc herniation, now likely aggravated or worsened by the landing.

After returning to Fort Bragg in 2011, I went through a more thorough evaluation at Womack Army Medical Center. They confirmed chronic lumbar disc disease with radiculopathy in my left leg, chronic pain, and persistent post-concussive symptoms from the 2008 blast. I was referred to behavioral health, where I was eventually diagnosed with PTSD related to multiple combat tours, including the IED strike and several firefights where we took casualties.

Over the next year, I went through physical therapy, pain management, and counseling. It became clear that continuing in a full infantry role, especially as an airborne NCO, wasn’t realistic. I had trouble running, jumping, and rucking without significant pain, and my headaches and concentration issues made it hard to perform at the level I expected from myself and that the Army needed.

As a result, I was medically boarded. My official separation from active duty was on 2012-09-30. My DD-214 lists my service from 2004-06-14 to 2012-09-30, with three deployments in support of Operation Iraqi Freedom and Operation Enduring Freedom. My primary injuries are:

1. Blast-related mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI) from IED strike on 2008-09-21 in Nangarhar Province, Afghanistan.
2. Chronic lumbar disc herniation at L4-L5 with radiculopathy, initially caused by the IED blast and further aggravated by airborne operations and a bad helo landing on 2010-11-03 in Kunar Province.
3. PTSD and related anxiety and sleep disturbances stemming from cumulative combat exposure across three deployments, including the 2008 IED incident and multiple engagements where we lost fellow soldiers.

Since leaving the Army, I’ve continued to receive care through the VA for these conditions. The back pain affects my daily life—simple things like playing with my kids on the floor or sitting through a long car ride can be difficult. The TBI and PTSD symptoms show up as memory lapses, headaches, trouble sleeping, and periods of irritability or withdrawal, especially around loud noises or crowded places.

To summarize, my post-9/11 service was eight years on active duty in the U.S. Army (2004-06-14 to 2012-09-30), with one tour in Iraq and two in Afghanistan. I was injured primarily by an IED blast on 2008-09-21 in Afghanistan, resulting in a mild traumatic brain injury and a lumbar spine injury, with a subsequent aggravation of the back injury in 2010. Those injuries, combined with the cumulative psychological toll of combat, ultimately led to my medical separation from the military.
